per_question.final_answer: The narrator contrasts brute-force and divide-and-conquer search strategies by describing brute-force as checking every item sequentially, as shown visually in the frame at 663.00s with the label "brute force" pointing to a linear array of numbers 1 through 9. The narrator contrasts this with divide-and-conquer, exemplified by binary search, which is implied to be more efficient (simplified down to O(n) in the transcript, though this appears to be an error as binary search is O(log n); the context suggests a comparison to brute-force's inefficiency). The concrete divide-and-conquer algorithm shown is depth-first-search, illustrated in the frame at 482.00s with a graph showing a path from "start" to "target" through nodes, including a "dead end." The comedic phrase used to describe brute-force speed is not explicitly stated in the provided evidence.

debug_info.gold_answer: The narrator says a brute-force approach 'would be to simply check every item until we find a target in a list,' but warns that 'if you want your program to run faster than a garden snail, you need a more sophisticated approach like divide and conquer.' The divide-and-conquer alternative shown is binary search: repeatedly check the middle element to see on which side the target lies, and keep searching in that half until you find it.

debug_info.gold_answer: 1) Identify the correct local.conf variable used by core images for extra packages: CORE_IMAGE_EXTRA_INSTALL. 2) Add 'bc' as a value to that variable in local.conf (the ad-hoc way, with the caveat that for reproducibility you would normally write it into a layer). 3) Re-run 'bitbake core-image-minimal' so the dependency 'bc' is built and pulled into the rebuilt image (he stresses you do not run 'bitbake bc' directly, because that only builds and packages bc, not the image).

debug_info.question: Summarize the speaker's explanation of the meaning of each subdirectory along the path build/tmp/deploy/images/qemux86 as he descends into it (around 12:30-13:30 of the video).
debug_info.gold_answer: 1) build is 'where we are starting to do funny things' (the build root). 2) tmp ('temp') is anything temporary that has no real value and can be deleted at any time without losing work — deleting it just costs more compilation time to recreate. 3) deploy is 'whatever falls out of the whole build process in the end' — usable artifacts you can take and use for some purpose. 4) images is the place where the bootable images live (he says 'we wanted to create an image that we can run and boot'). 5) qemux86 is 'the default architecture if you're doing a build', as also documented in the quick start manual.

debug_info.question: Summarize the speaker's argument for why fixtures are necessary, including the concrete failing scenario he demonstrates after he removes the user_manager fixture.
debug_info.gold_answer: The speaker argues that tests should run in isolation: each test should start from a fresh state, otherwise side effects from one test can affect another. To demonstrate, he comments out the @pytest.fixture user_manager and replaces it with a global `user_manager = UserManager()`. When he re-runs pytest, test_add_duplicate_user fails because the previous test_add_user already added 'john_doe' to the global UserManager, so the new add in the duplicate test triggers a failure not because the duplicate-detection logic is broken but because of cross-test state leakage. Restoring the fixture fixes the issue by giving each test a freshly constructed UserManager.

debug_info.question: In what order are the three historical applications of Markov chains presented in the video, and roughly when does each section begin?
debug_info.gold_answer: The video presents three applications in this order: (1) the Monte Carlo method for nuclear bomb simulations starting around 9:55 (~595s), introduced via the 16 July 1945 Trinity 'gadget' detonation; (2) Google PageRank for web search starting around 16:35 (~995s), introduced via the rise of the early web in 1993; and (3) language modeling / next-token prediction with Claude Shannon and modern LLMs starting around 25:14 (~1515s).

debug_info.question: List, in the order Markov performs them, the steps the video says he took to build his vowel/consonant predictive machine from Pushkin's poem.
debug_info.gold_answer: 1) Take the first 20,000 letters of Eugene Onegin, strip out punctuation and spaces, and concatenate into one long string. 2) Count the letters and find that 43% are vowels and 57% are consonants. 3) Break the string into overlapping pairs (VV, CV, CC, VC) and count their actual frequencies (e.g., VV pairs occur ~6% of the time vs. ~18% expected if independent). 4) Draw two states (one vowel circle, one consonant circle) and connect them with arrows for each transition. 5) Compute transition probabilities by dividing pair frequencies by single-letter frequencies (e.g., 0.06/0.43 ≈ 13% for V->V, so 87% for V->C). 6) Run the chain by sampling random numbers between 0 and 1 against the thresholds, generating letters and tracking the running ratio until it converges to 43%/57%.

debug_info.question: Summarize how PageRank is illustrated in the toy four-page web (Amy, Ben, Chris, Dan): which transitions are highlighted, what extra rule is added to handle disconnected pages, and what is the named damping value?
debug_info.gold_answer: Four pages (Amy, Ben, Chris, Dan) are drawn as nodes. Amy links only to Ben (a 100% transition from Amy to Ben). Ben links to Amy, Chris and Dan (each with a 1/3 = 33% transition probability). The other transitions are filled in similarly. A simulated random surfer starts on a page and the long-run share of time on each page becomes that page's rank. To avoid getting stuck in loops on disconnected subgraphs, the algorithm adds a damping rule: 85% of the time the surfer follows a link as normal, and 15% of the time they jump to a random page on the web.

debug_info.question: Summarize the central feud between Markov and Nekrasov as the video frames it: what specific claim of Nekrasov's was Markov refuting, and what concrete experiment with Pushkin's poem closed the argument?
debug_info.gold_answer: Nekrasov claimed that observing the law of large numbers in social statistics (e.g., Belgian marriage rates, crime rates) implied the underlying decisions must be independent, and therefore evidence of free will. Markov refuted this by constructing a system whose events are explicitly dependent yet still obey the law of large numbers. He counted the first 20,000 letters of Pushkin's Eugene Onegin (43% vowels, 57% consonants), measured pair frequencies that disagreed with independence (e.g., VV pairs at 6% vs. ~18% if independent), built a two-state Markov chain with the derived transition probabilities (V->V ~13%, V->C 87%), ran it, and showed the running vowel/consonant ratio still converges to 43/57. This proved convergence to expected values does not imply independence, dismantling Nekrasov's free-will argument.

debug_info.question: List, in order, the steps the speaker walks through for the Two Sum solution using a hash map.
debug_info.gold_answer: 1) Create an empty hash map called `numToIndex` to store each number with its index. 2) Loop through the array using `enumerate`, getting both index `i` and current number `num`. 3) Compute the complement = `target - num`. 4) Check whether `complement` is already in `numToIndex`. 5) If yes, return the pair of indices (the stored complement's index and `i`). 6) Otherwise, store `numToIndex[num] = i` and continue.

debug_info.question: Summarize how the video extends vanilla binary search to the 'monotonic condition' template: what assumption replaces 'sorted array', what does the boolean function need to satisfy, and what does the on-screen monotonic vs non-monotonic graph illustrate?
debug_info.gold_answer: Vanilla binary search requires a sorted array so it can discard one half each step. The video generalizes this: instead of 'sorted', the only requirement is a monotonic condition - a function f(x) that, once it transitions in one direction, never reverses (e.g., once it returns True it stays True). Given a feasibility function `is_index_valid(mid)`, you can binary-search the first True index in O(log n). The monotonic vs non-monotonic graph (around 2500s) illustrates this with a blue monotonic curve and a red non-monotonic curve passing through (x=5, y=100); on the monotonic curve every value to the left of (5, 100) is provably smaller and every value to the right is larger, so binary search can cut the search space in half - whereas on the non-monotonic curve no such guarantee exists, so binary search does not apply.

per_question.final_answer: The trick used to convert Python's default min heap into a max heap is to negate all the numbers. The operations called are: first, negate all numbers; then, push them into the heap; and finally, pop the smallest element (which corresponds to the largest original number). The final return statement is the negated value popped from the heap.

debug_info.question: Summarize the K-th largest element using a heap as walked through at the end of the video: what trick is used to convert Python's default min heap into a max heap, what operations are called and in what order, and what is the final return statement?
debug_info.gold_answer: The trick is to negate every number so that the smallest negated value is the largest original value, turning Python's default min heap into a max heap. The procedure is: 1) negate all numbers in the input list; 2) call `heapify` on the negated list (linear time); 3) pop from the heap k-1 times (each pop yields the current largest negated value); 4) return the value now at the top of the heap, but flip the sign back to recover the original positive value. The result is the k-th largest element without sorting the entire array.

debug_info.question: List, in order, the steps the video says a miner performs to create and broadcast a new block in the Bitcoin protocol.
debug_info.gold_answer: The narrator's own summary (at ~1109-1122s) describes four steps a miner performs: 1) Listening for transactions being broadcast. 2) Creating blocks (which includes collecting transactions into a block, adding a special block-reward transaction at the top, and finding a proof-of-work nonce). 3) Broadcasting those completed blocks to the network. 4) Getting rewarded with new money for doing so. Separately, the video also explains that each block must include the hash of the previous block in its header (to chain blocks together) and that other participants verify the proof of work before appending it to their own copy of the blockchain.

The transcript excerpts discuss hash functions (SHA-256), block validation, and conflicting blockchains, and the frames show visualizations of conflicting chains and cryptographic signatures, but none explicitly address the statistical infeasibility of a 50% attack or Bob's defense rule. Therefore, the answer cannot be determined from the given evidence.

debug_info.question: Summarize the video's argument for why a 50%-attack scenario is statistically infeasible as long as Alice has less than ~50% of the network's compute, including what Bob's defense rule is.
debug_info.gold_answer: Alice tries to fool Bob with a fraudulent block in which she pays him without broadcasting that block to the rest of the network, then keeps secretly extending that fork. To succeed she would have to find each successive proof-of-work block faster than the rest of the miners combined. Bob's defense rule, derived from the protocol, is to always trust the longest chain he knows about. As long as Alice has less than ~50% of the network's computing power, the probability becomes overwhelming that the honest miners' chain grows faster than Alice's fraudulent fork; eventually Bob will see the longer honest chain and reject Alice's. So the safety advice is to wait for several additional blocks on top of any block before trusting it.

per_question.final_answer: The provided evidence does not contain sufficient information to summarize how new Bitcoin enters the economy, including details about the initial reward, the halving schedule, or the maximum supply implication. The transcript excerpts discuss general concepts about cryptocurrencies and mention that the video is not comprehensive, while the frame evidence only shows a diagram of the Bitcoin protocol and user-facing aspects without any specific details about Bitcoin's monetary issuance mechanics.

debug_info.question: Summarize how new bitcoin enters the economy according to the video, including the initial reward, the halving schedule, and the maximum supply implication.
debug_info.gold_answer: All new bitcoin enters the economy through block rewards: when a miner successfully finds a valid proof of work for a new block, they include a special transaction at the top of that block giving themselves some bitcoin out of thin air (no signature required). Initially this reward was 50 BTC per block. Every 210,000 blocks (about every four years) the reward is cut in half; at the time of the video the reward is 12.5 BTC per block. Because the reward decreases geometrically, the total bitcoin in existence is capped at 21 million. After block rewards become negligible, miners are incentivized via optional per-transaction fees that senders attach to encourage inclusion in the next block.

debug_info.question: Summarize how Textual's three CSS targeting selectors are used in the 'styled hello world' demo: which selector applies to which widget(s), and what concrete visual difference does each produce in the rendered app?
debug_info.gold_answer: The three selectors are: 1) the Python-class selector `Static { background: blue; padding: 1 1; }` which applies to ALL Static widgets and gives them a blue background plus 1x1 padding. 2) The id selector `#static_with_id { border: solid white; }` which applies only to the second widget (the one yielded with `id="static_with_id"`) and adds a solid white border. 3) The CSS-class selectors `.static_cls1 { background: green; }` and `.static_cls2 { border: dashed yellow; }`, which apply to widgets via the `classes=` argument; the third widget (`classes="static_cls1"`) becomes green-backed (overriding blue), and the fourth widget (`classes="static_cls1 static_cls2"`) is green AND gets a dashed yellow border. Visually the four widgets appear stacked: a plain blue 'A static widget', a blue 'Using an id' with a solid white border, a plain green 'First class', and a green 'Second class' inside a dashed yellow border.

debug_info.question: Summarize Textual's event-handling model as introduced in the video: which two specific 'on_*' handlers does the speaker explain first, what triggers each, and what concrete change does the speaker make so that pressing 'q' exits the app?
debug_info.gold_answer: The first two handlers introduced are `on_mount` and `on_key`. `on_mount` fires after the compose method, once all widgets exist and Textual has resolved sizes; the speaker uses it to programmatically set styles like background, border, padding, and margin on the Static and Label widgets. `on_key` fires for every key press; the speaker overrides it with a Python 3.10 pattern-matching `match event.key:` block, and adds `case "q": exit()` so that pressing 'q' calls `exit()` and quits the app (replacing the default Ctrl+Q quit shortcut he dislikes).

debug_info.gold_answer: 1) Open a new VS Code terminal with Ctrl+Shift+Backtick. 2) Type `git init` and press Enter. 3) Click the dropdown arrow next to the terminal selector and choose 'Command Prompt' to open a Command Prompt terminal. 4) Type `claude` and press Enter to launch Claude Code. 5) Press '1' to trust the directory.

debug_info.question: When the speaker enters YOLO mode (around 12:30) the screen at 750s shows a red warning box. What exact heading and the two prompt options does the box display, and what command did the speaker type to trigger this warning?
debug_info.gold_answer: The warning header reads 'WARNING: Claude Code running in Bypass Permissions mode'. Body text explains that 'In Bypass Permissions mode, Claude Code will not ask for your approval before running potentially dangerous commands. This mode should only be used in a sandboxed container/VM that has restricted internet access and can easily be restored if damaged. By proceeding, you accept all responsibility for actions taken while running in Bypass Permissions mode.' The two visible options are '1. No, exit' and '2. Yes, I accept'. The triggering command on the previous line is `C:\src\scratch>claude --dangerously-skip-permissions`.

debug_info.gold_answer: 1) The speaker switches to scenario 3 (existing-app architecture change). 2) He launches Claude in YOLO mode (`claude --dangerously-skip-permissions`) and accepts the bypass-permissions warning. 3) He toggles into plan mode with Shift+Tab. 4) The prompt explains the desired architecture change (changes should automatically sync across tabs without refresh) and asks Claude to explore relevant files and give three options. 5) Claude returns three options: two browser-sync solutions plus a third option to create a server and do real-time syncing across clients; the speaker picks option 3. 6) Claude returns a multi-phase plan (Phase 1: Server setup with five sub-steps including ws/typescript/tsx dependencies, stateStore, WebSocket handler, entry point; Phase 2: Client sync service with WebSocket connection management, exponential-backoff reconnection, offline action queue, and a useSync React hook; Phase 3: AppContext integration). 7) The speaker accepts the plan (option 1) and lets YOLO mode run unattended for ~5 minutes. 8) Claude builds the WebSocket server and explains how it works. 9) The speaker validates by running two browser instances side-by-side: adding a team member 'Elizabeth' (red) appears in the second browser instantly; adding the chore 'Feed the Fish' assigned to her also appears instantly. 10) He recommends reviewing the resulting code before trusting it.

3.  $T$ (the time to maturity)
4.  $r$ (the risk-free interest rate)
5.  $q$ (the dividend yield)

Based on the provided evidence, at the frame corresponding to 900s (which is frame 847.00-848.00s), the speaker writes down the following two named ingredients for the 'New VIX':

1.  **L. Scott's "Log Contract"** (associated with the year **ca. 1987**)
2.  **New CRR-Merton Formula** (associated with the year **ca. 1990**)

The specific value that the log contract pays at maturity T is written as **e^T * E[ln(S_T)]**.

The evidence does not explicitly state the year associated with the "New CRR-Merton Formula" as "ca. 1990" in the frame at 900s, but the transcript segment at 751.97-755.73s mentions "in the 1990s" in the context of the VIX, which aligns with the "New VIX" being introduced. debug_info.gold_answer: Around 12:00 (~720s), after critiquing the old VIX. He explains that when markets go down people panic and volatility goes up; when markets go up volatility goes down. So when VIX is high, people are panicked (advisors say it's a good time to buy because prices are 'irrationally low'); when VIX is low, people are 'complacent' (so 'maybe don't do anything').

debug_info.question: List, in the order presented in the video, the steps the CBOE uses to build the OLD VIX from market option prices for a target 30-day horizon.
debug_info.gold_answer: 1) For a given option maturity, find the strikes nearest the forward index level. 2) Compute Black-Scholes implied volatility for the at-the-forward call and put separately. 3) Average the call and put implied volatilities to get the at-the-forward implied volatility for that maturity (sigma_K). 4) Because there is rarely an exact 30-day expiration, find the two expirations T1 and T2 that bracket the 30-day point and compute sigma_{T1} and sigma_{T2}. 5) Convert each annualized vol to total variance over its time period: sigma_{Ti}^2 * Ti. 6) Time-linearly interpolate variance to the 30-day point using the coefficients (T2-T)/(T2-T1) and (T-T1)/(T2-T1). 7) Solve for sigma_T: divide by T and take the square root. 8) Old VIX = 100 * sigma_T.

debug_info.question: Summarize, with the two explicitly labeled assumptions (plus the square-integrability requirement) and the two main 'discoveries', why the new VIX (post-2002) replaced the old VIX, and what concrete object the new VIX is mathematically computing.
debug_info.gold_answer: The old VIX (pre-2002) was an at-the-forward Black-Scholes implied volatility derived by averaging call and put implied vols at the strike nearest the forward, then time-linearly interpolating variance to a 30-day horizon. Two issues motivated replacement: (a) Black-Scholes is empirically wrong (different strikes have different implied vols - the volatility smile), so the at-the-forward number is not specially meaningful; (b) demand for futures/options on the old VIX never materialized. The new VIX leverages two 1990s discoveries: Lewis Scott's log-contract (a derivative paying ln(S_T) at T, priced as e^{-rT} E[ln(S_T)]) and the Carr-Madan formula (any twice-differentiable payoff can be replicated as a linear combination of OTM puts and calls plus hockey-sticks). The derivation relies on two explicitly labeled assumptions -- Assumption 1: S follows a stochastic-vol diffusion under the risk-neutral measure, and Assumption 2: sigma_t is non-anticipating w.r.t. dW_t -- plus the side requirement that sigma_t sample paths be square-integrable. Together these yield a closed-form expression for the expected future realized variance over [0,T] in terms of OTM put and call prices. The new VIX is therefore 100 * sqrt(sigma_T^2) where sigma_T^2 is this expected future realized annualized variance, time-linearly interpolated to a 30-day horizon - a model-free measure of expected future realized variance, not just an at-the-forward implied vol.

The speaker's chain of reasoning, as shown in the evidence, proceeds as follows:

1.  **Starting Point (Lewis Scott Log Contract):** The speaker begins by discussing the Lewis Scott log contract, which is a financial instrument designed to provide exposure to the expected future realized variance. The derivation involves stochastic calculus, including terms related to Brownian motion increments (as seen in frames 1372 and 2761, and referenced in the transcript at 1333.21-1341.45s).

2.  **Conversion to European Options (Carr-Madan):** The speaker then explains that the Carr-Madan approach converts the log contract into a combination of European call and put option prices. This is explicitly stated in the transcript at 3222.58-3228.02s: "the market and says, well, we know these are European options. So put call parity applies." The board at 3267 (frame 3267) visually reinforces this by showing the application of put-call parity (PCP) to find the forward price (F) from market put and call prices: `F = K + e^(-rT)[

debug_info.question: Summarize the speaker's chain of reasoning from the Lewis Scott log contract (board at 900s) to a tradeable formula for expected future realized variance, including how Carr-Madan converts the log contract into call and put prices.
debug_info.gold_answer: 1) Lewis Scott (1987): consider the European derivative paying ln(S_T) at maturity T; its no-arbitrage price is e^{-rT} \hat E[ln(S_T)] under the risk-neutral measure. 2) Apply Ito's lemma (Taylor expansion) to ln(S_T) under the stochastic-volatility SDE dS_t = (r-q) S_t dt + sigma_t S_t dW_t to write ln(S_T) = ln(F) + integral sigma_t dW_t - (1/2) integral sigma_t^2 dt. 3) Take risk-neutral expectations using assumption 2 (sigma_t non-anticipating w.r.t. dW), so E[integral sigma_t dW_t] = 0. This gives \hat E[ln(S_T)] = ln F - (1/2) \hat E[integral_0^T sigma_t^2 dt]. 4) Rearrange so the expected future realized variance integral_0^T sigma_t^2 dt = -2 (\hat E[ln(S_T)] - ln F) = -2 \hat E[ln(S_T/F)]. 5) Apply the Carr-Madan formula to f(x) = ln(x/F) (a twice-differentiable payoff) to express ln(S_T/F) as a linear combination of OTM puts (for K < F) and OTM calls (for K > F), plus a hockey-stick at the boundary. 6) Take expectations: E[OTM call payoff at K] e^{-rT} = call price C(K), and likewise for puts. The result is an integral over strikes K of [P(K) for K<F or C(K) for K>F] / K^2. 7) The expected future realized variance over [0,T] then equals (2/T) * e^{rT} * integral_0^infty Q(K)/K^2 dK plus a small correction from the (F/K_0 - 1)^2 Taylor term. 8) Time-linearly interpolate to a 30-day horizon and define the new VIX = 100 * sqrt(sigma_T^2).

debug_info.question: What is the ordered sequence of file/class creations Joel performs to build the library, from project bootstrap through training, before he writes any end-to-end example?
debug_info.gold_answer: 1) Create the project root `JoelNet/` (with README, .gitignore, pylintrc). 2) Create the inner package directory `JoelNet/JoelNet/` and add `__init__.py`. 3) Create `tensor.py` and alias `Tensor = numpy.ndarray` (`from numpy import ndarray as Tensor`). 4) Create `loss.py` with abstract `Loss` class (`loss` and `grad` methods) and concrete `MSE`. 5) Create `layers.py` with abstract `Layer` (`forward`/`backward`), concrete `Linear` (W, b in `params`), and `Activation` plus `Tanh`. 6) Create `nn.py` with `NeuralNet` class that holds a list of layers and implements `forward` (sequential pass) and `backward` (reversed pass). 7) Create `optim.py` with abstract `Optimizer` and concrete `SGD(lr=0.01)` whose `step` does `param -= lr * grad`. 8) Create `data.py` with `DataIterator` and `BatchIterator` (yielding namedtuple `Batch(inputs, targets)`). 9) Create `train.py` with the `train(net, inputs, targets, num_epochs=5000, iterator=BatchIterator(), loss=MSE(), optimizer=SGD())` loop.

debug_info.question: Summarize what XOR demonstrates in this video, including the failure mode with a single linear layer and the specific architecture change Joel makes to fix it.
debug_info.gold_answer: XOR is presented as the canonical 'cannot be learned by a linear function' problem (not linearly separable). Joel first defines inputs np.array([[0,0],[1,0],[0,1],[1,1]]) and one-hot targets, then uses a single linear layer mapping 2->2. After 5000 epochs the loss collapses to 0 and predictions are 0.5/0.5 - useless - because a single linear layer cannot represent XOR. He then changes the network to add a hidden non-linearity: NeuralNet([Linear(input_size=2, output_size=2), Tanh(), Linear(input_size=2, output_size=2)]) - i.e., a hidden layer of 2 units with a Tanh activation. Now over 5000 epochs the loss becomes very small and predictions approach the correct one-hot outputs (close to [1,0] for 0,0 and 1,1 and close to [0,1] for 0,1 and 1,0).

debug_info.question: Summarize, with references to specific files and helpers, how the FizzBuzz example uses the library end-to-end and what trick is used to encode integer inputs and one-hot outputs.
debug_info.gold_answer: 1) Joel writes a `fizz_buzz_encode(x)` helper returning a 4-element one-hot list ([1,0,0,0], [0,1,0,0] (Fizz), [0,0,1,0] (Buzz), [0,0,0,1] (FizzBuzz)) based on x % 15, x % 5, x % 3. 2) He writes a `binary_encode(x: int) -> List[int]` using `return [x >> i & 1 for i in range(10)]` - a 10-digit binary encoding, motivated as a 'common interview trick'. 3) Training data is `inputs = np.array([binary_encode(x) for x in range(101, 1024)])` and `targets = np.array([fizz_buzz_encode(x) for x in range(101, 1024)])` - i.e., train on numbers above 100 so the model is tested on 1-100 (it has not seen them). 4) The model is `NeuralNet([Linear(input_size=10, output_size=50), Tanh(), Linear(input_size=50, output_size=4)])` - 50 hidden units chosen empirically as 'a nice trade off between mostly works and doesn't take forever to train'. 5) On the first run the loss overflows to NaN with the default SGD learning rate of 0.01; the fix is to pass `optimizer=SGD(learning_rate=0.001)` to `train(...)`. 6) After 5000 epochs the network is run on x in range(1, 101); for each x he picks `argmax(predicted)` and prints the corresponding label ('Fizz', 'Buzz', 'FizzBuzz', or x).
